01a_quotes.title: Impact of the Availability of Chat-GPT on Software Development Activity

study_design: Utilizing natural experiments where some governments banned ChatGPT, we employ Difference-in-Differences (DID), Synthetic Control (SC), and Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SDID) methods to estimate its effects.

To make this measurement, we can exploit the fact that, initially, ChatGPT was made available only in a subset of countries, which can be considered a treatment group; the remaining groups can serve as a control group.
With the availability of data on software development at the country level, we have the necessary pieces to analyze the availability of ChatGPT as a treatment in a natural experiment.

We use the Difference in Differences, Synthetic Control, and Synthetic Difference in Differences approaches to analyzing quasi-experimental data to estimate ChatGPT's effect on software production in each country-quarter.
This design consists of 120 treatment units and 27 control units; 11 pre-treatment periods and 2 treatment periods.
task: The entirety of the data come from GitHub's Innovation Graph, specifically the table that contains the number of pushes per country and per quarter.

We also conducted a similar analysis at the country-programming language level, examining the number of unique developers in each economy who made at least one git push to a repository with a given programming language.
population: The data spans form the first quarter of 2020 until the first quarter of 2023.
The information is also separated by jurisdiction, including a total of 179 units.
Of these, we keep 151 after dropping 28 units that lack observations for the whole time span.
We also remove the observations related the EU as an aggregate, as they cause perfectly collinear with those of the individual EU countries.
Finally, we also exclude Hong Kong from our analysis, because it shows an atypically high level of pushes compared to all other countries.
arms: The treated units are the countries that were originally announced to have access to the application, and during the time span included, no new countries were added to the treatment group.
We consider the start of the treatment period to be the fourth quarter of 2022, as the initial launch of the app came out on November 30th of the same year.
outcome_definitions: Panel A outcome is The number of times developers in a given economy uploaded code to GitHub per 100,000 people.

This metric includes all instances where developers pushed code changes to repositories, whether through the command line or GitHub's online platform, with each push potentially containing multiple commits.
Panel B outcome is The number of software projects (repositories) in a given economy per 100,000 people.
This metric is based on the modal location of all repository members with triage and above access.
It includes all repositories, regardless of whether they are actively developed or maintained.
Panel C outcome is The number of developer accounts located in a given economy per 100,000 people, based on the modal daily location.
This metric excludes bot accounts and users flagged as "spammy" within internal systems.
It includes developer accounts that may no longer be active.
All panels measure the number of unique developers in each economy who made at least one git push to a repository with a given programming language.
This measure includes all developers who contributed code, regardless of the size or number of contributions, as long as they made at least one push to a repository.
statistical_results: The DID estimation shows an increase of approximately 899.268 pushes, which is highly significant with a standard error of 147.395.
Both the SC and SDID methods also show positive impacts, with coefficients of 595.059 and 645.623 respectively, though the SC estimate is not statistically significant.
The baseline mean outcome is 741.5 pushes per 100,000, suggesting that the introduction of ChatGPT has significantly boosted developer activity in terms of code submissions.
The DID estimate shows a substantial and statistically significant increase of 1657.141 repositories, indicating a robust positive effect.
However, the SC and SDID estimates, while still positive (464.735 and 204.734 respectively), are not statistically significant.

The baseline mean outcome for this measure is 1097.8 repositories per 100,000 people, suggesting that while ChatGPT has a pronounced impact on repository creation as per the DID analysis, the other methods do not confirm the same level of significance.
The DID results indicate a significant increase of 578.912 developers, with a standard error of 109.398, highlighting a substantial positive impact.
In contrast, the SC and SDID methods show much smaller effects (13.752 and 51.693 respectively), with neither reaching statistical significance.

The baseline mean outcome is 527.4 developers per 100,000, suggesting that the presence of ChatGPT has potentially encouraged more individuals to engage in development activities, though the robustness of this finding varies by method.

ambiguity_or_caveats: One result shown by Abadie, Diamond and Hainmueller (2010) Abadie et al. (2010) for the Synthetic Control estimator – which also holds for the SDiD estimator – is that the upper bound of the magnitude of the estimation bias for the Average Treatment Effect grows with the number of control units and shrinks with the number of pre-treatment periods.

Overall, the results suggest that the introduction of ChatGPT has generally positive effects on software development activities, particularly in increasing the number of git pushes and repositories created, as evidenced by the DID estimates.
However, the magnitude and statistical significance of these effects vary across different methodological approaches.

These findings imply that while ChatGPT has likely enhanced developer productivity and engagement, further analysis may be necessary to fully understand the extent and consistency of its impact across different contexts.
There are potential concerns regarding the quality of code generated by ChatGPT, as it is trained on diverse sources, some of which may not contain high-quality code.
This could result in increased code churn, potentially offsetting the benefits of faster code creation.
While current evidence primarily highlights productivity gains through collaborative code writing, future research should also explore the effects of AI availability through other channels.
This includes examining the long-term impacts on code quality, developer skills, and the broader economic and social implications of widespread AI tool usage.

01a_quotes.notes: This is an empirical study using natural experiments (government bans of ChatGPT) to estimate causal effects on software development activity. The paper employs three quasi-experimental methods (DID, SC, SDID) and reports results across multiple outcomes (pushes, repositories, developers) and programming languages. Results are presented at both the aggregate country level and the country-programming language level.

01_extraction.title: Impact of the Availability of Chat-GPT on Software Development Activity
01_extraction.study_design_description: This study utilizes natural experiments where some governments banned ChatGPT to estimate its effects on software development. The design consists of 120 treatment units and 27 control units, with 11 pre-treatment periods and 2 treatment periods. The authors employ Difference-in-Differences (DID), Synthetic Control (SC), and Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SDID) methods to analyze quasi-experimental data and estimate ChatGPT's effect on software production in each country-quarter.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: The study analyzes software development activity using data from GitHub's Innovation Graph, specifically examining the number of pushes per country and per quarter, as well as the number of unique developers in each economy who made at least one git push to a repository with a given programming language.
01_extraction.population_description: The data spans from the first quarter of 2020 until the first quarter of 2023, covering 151 country units (after dropping 28 units that lacked observations for the whole time span, excluding the EU aggregate and Hong Kong). The treatment period begins in the fourth quarter of 2022, when ChatGPT was initially launched on November 30th.

outcomes.outcome_description: The number of times developers in a given economy uploaded code to GitHub per 100,000 people. This metric includes all instances where developers pushed code changes to repositories, whether through the command line or GitHub's online platform, with each push potentially containing multiple commits.

The number of software projects (repositories) in a given economy per 100,000 people. This metric is based on the modal location of all repository members with triage and above access. It includes all repositories, regardless of whether they are actively developed or maintained.

The number of developer accounts located in a given economy per 100,000 people, based on the modal daily location. This metric excludes bot accounts and users flagged as "spammy" within internal systems. It includes developer accounts that may no longer be active.

01_extraction.notes: This is a natural experiment study exploiting government bans of ChatGPT to estimate causal effects on software development activity. The paper employs three quasi-experimental methods (DID, Synthetic Control, and Synthetic Difference-in-Differences) and reports results across multiple outcomes at both the aggregate country level and the country-programming language level. The study design consists of 120 treatment units (countries with ChatGPT access) and 27 control units (countries without access), with 11 pre-treatment periods and 2 treatment periods. The treatment period begins in Q4 2022 when ChatGPT was launched on November 30th. Results show generally positive effects of ChatGPT availability on code pushes, repositories, and developers across most programming languages, though the magnitude and statistical significance vary by method. One notable exception is TSQL, which shows a negative DID coefficient. The authors note potential concerns about code quality and suggest future research should examine long-term impacts on code quality, developer skills, and broader economic implications.

02_method.rationale: The study explicitly states it 'utilizes natural experiments where some governments banned ChatGPT' and employs quasi-experimental methods including 'Difference-in-Differences (DID), Synthetic Control (SC), and Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SDID) methods to analyze quasi-experimental data.' This is a clear natural experiment design leveraging policy variation across countries.

04_onet.rationale: The study's participants are software developers performing 'git push' operations to GitHub repositories across different programming languages. The verbatim task description states they are examining 'the number of pushes per country' and 'unique developers...who made at least one git push to a repository with a given programming language.' This is core software development work—writing code, version control, and repository management—which is fundamentally computer-based work. While 'Software Developers' (15-1252.00) exists as an occupation, the task here is narrowly defined as the specific activity of pushing code to repositories, making the work activity mapping more appropriate and generalizable.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: This is a natural experiment comparing countries where ChatGPT was available vs. banned. The treatment group consists of 120 countries with ChatGPT access, control group is 27 countries without access.
llm_extracted.notes: Primary outcome is 'Num Pushes per 100k' (git pushes per 100,000 people). Table 6.1 Panel A reports DID coefficient of 899.268*** (SE=147.395) with baseline mean of 741.5. This is a level-transformed outcome (not log), so the coefficient represents the absolute increase in pushes per 100k. The baseline mean is explicitly stated as 'Baseline Mean Outcome 741.5'. The outcome is a throughput measure (more pushes = more productivity), so larger_is_better. Sample sizes: N0=27 control units, N1=120 treatment units across 13 time periods (11 pre-treatment, 2 post-treatment), total observations=2352. SC and SDID methods also reported but DID is most straightforward for extraction.

This design consists of 120 treatment units and 27 control units; 11 pre-treatment periods and 2 treatment periods.

Panel A outcome is The number of times developers in a given economy uploaded code to GitHub per 100,000 people.

computed.notes: Primary outcome is 'Num Pushes per 100k' (git pushes per 100,000 people). Table 6.1 Panel A reports DID coefficient of 899.268*** (SE=147.395) with baseline mean of 741.5. This is a level-transformed outcome (not log), so the coefficient represents the absolute increase in pushes per 100k. The baseline mean is explicitly stated as 'Baseline Mean Outcome 741.5'. The outcome is a throughput measure (more pushes = more productivity), so larger_is_better. Sample sizes: N0=27 control units, N1=120 treatment units across 13 time periods (11 pre-treatment, 2 post-treatment), total observations=2352. SC and SDID methods also reported but DID is most straightforward for extraction.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: This is a natural experiment comparing countries where ChatGPT was available (120 treatment units) versus countries where it was banned/unavailable (27 control units). The treatment is ChatGPT availability starting Q4 2022.
llm_extracted.notes: This is a country-level quasi-experimental study using DID, SC, and SDID methods. The paper reports treatment effects (ATEs) directly rather than raw means/SDs. For Panel A (Num Pushes per 100k), DID coefficient = 899.268*** (SE=147.395), SC = 595.059 (SE=437.061), SDID = 645.623*** (SE=146.445). Baseline mean = 741.5. The reported coefficients are regression-based treatment effects, not standardized effect sizes. Sample sizes are n_control=27 countries, n_treatment=120 countries, with 11 pre-treatment periods and 2 post-treatment periods (total observations=2352). Without raw means and SDs for the treatment and control groups in the post-treatment period, or a reported standardized effect size, we cannot compute Hedges' g. The paper uses panel data methods that account for time trends, making simple mean comparisons inappropriate. The study design is fundamentally different from a standard RCT - it's a difference-in-differences analysis at the country-quarter level.
source_quotes: Panel A. Num Pushes per 100k: Chat GPT Available 899.268*** (147.395), Observations 2352, Baseline Mean Outcome 741.5
This design consists of 120 treatment units and 27 control units; 11 pre-treatment periods and 2 treatment periods.
The treated units are the countries that were originally announced to have access to the application
We consider the start of the treatment period to be the fourth quarter of 2022, as the initial launch of the app came out on November 30th of the same year.
